MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar May-06-2025 Continued To Feb-09-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar May-12-2025 Continued To Dec-10-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301

PLAINTIFF MACARTHUR MCDANIEL'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO AMEND PLAINTIFF'S COMPLAINT (TENATATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar May-05-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Plaintiff'S Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar May-05-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF MACARTHUR MCDANIEL'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO Amend Plaintiff'S Complaint

Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is denied.

The court's reasoning is as follows. Plaintiff's proposed amendment does not seek to add causes of action but merely to plead facts and legal theories that support a contention that defendant was grossly negligent or reckless. The amendment to the complaint is unnecessary for plaintiff to make those arguments. "California does not recognize a distinct cause of action for 'gross negligence' independent of a statutory basis." (Saenz v. Whitewater Voyages, Inc. (1990) 226 Cal.App.3d 758, 766, fn. 9; see also Continental Ins. Co. v. American Protection Industries (1987) 197 Cal.App.3d 322, 328, 330 ["Do Our Courts Recognize a Cause of Action for 'Gross Negligence'? No...[I]n light of the adoption of the doctrine of comparative negligence in California, any attempt to categorize gross negligence separately from ordinary negligence is unnecessary."].) Instead, gross negligence is a species of negligence that is already put at issue by plaintiff's negligence cause of action. (See Hass v. RhodyCo Productions (2018) 26 Cal.App.5th 11, 32 ["regardless of whether gross negligence can be a separate cause of action, and/or the Hass Family could have alleged gross negligence in the Complaint in anticipation of RhodyCo's likely defense, they were not required to do so"].)

Because plaintiff did not plead around the release, the City is not required to refute the gross negligence theory in order to meet its initial burden of production on summary judgment. Plaintiff may rely on gross negligence to create a triable issue of fact in opposition to the summary judgment motion. (See Hass, supra, at p. 33.)

Thus, there is no effect on plaintiff's claims or summary judgment position by the court's denial of the motion, nor would plaintiff be precluded at trial from presenting evidence of gross negligence. But the effect on the City would be to moot its summary judgment motion, and would preclude it from moving for summary judgment entirely in light of the approaching trial date of July 14, 2025.

This order is without prejudice to a renewed motion to amend in the event the trial date is continued. Motions to continue trial are heard in Department 206 by the Presiding Judge (Local Rule 6.0(B)), and this court expresses no view on the merits of a motion to continue trial.

Order is signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
